title: Preventing egg eating by hens
subtitle: Why hens start eating their own eggs and what changes the habit before it spreads through the flock.
excerpt: Egg eating usually starts with an accident (a thin-shelled egg that breaks on the nest floor) and quickly becomes a learned behaviour. Once a hen discovers that eggs are edible the habit is hard to break, and it spreads to flock-mates within days. Addressing the root causes and the nest-box setup stops most outbreaks before they establish.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Code of Practice for the Welfare of Laying Hens (2018). Lewis Wright, The Practical Poultry Keeper (1867), Internet Archive.

glossaryTerms.definition: A nest box with a sloped floor that rolls each egg into a covered collection tray once laid, keeping it out of reach of the hen. The most reliable physical prevention for persistent egg eaters.

An egg with a shell too thin to survive normal handling, often caused by calcium deficiency, heat stress, or old hens. Thin-shelled eggs crack easily in the nest box, creating the first opportunity for a hen to taste egg contents.

content.text: Egg eating in a chicken flock almost always traces back to a broken egg. The first crack is usually accidental: a
thin-shelled egg

breaking on landing, an overcrowded box with hens trampling each other's eggs, or old nest-box litter too hard to cushion the fall. Once a hen tastes the contents, the association is made. The habit spreads fast because chickens learn by watching each other.
Address the root cause first
Before changing the nest boxes, identify why eggs are breaking. The most common causes:
Calcium deficiency: shells are thin and fragile. Offer oyster shell in a separate free-choice feeder. See
feeding laying hens
for the right grit and supplement setup.
Old or compacted nest litter: worn-down shavings provide no cushioning. Refresh the boxes with 10-15 cm of clean wood shavings.
Too few nest boxes: hens queue and jostle, breaking eggs underfoot. Allow one box per three to four hens.
Overcrowding or boredom: hens with insufficient space and enrichment explore and peck at anything in the box.
Physical nest box changes

Collect eggs frequently: two or three times a day during the main laying period removes the opportunity before habits form. Most hens lay in the morning; collecting at midday and again in the late afternoon covers the peak.
Fitting a
rollaway nest

box is the most reliable physical solution. The sloped floor rolls the egg into a covered tray the moment it is laid, out of sight and out of reach. Persistent egg eaters find nothing to work on and the habit typically extinguishes within a week.
Darkening the nest boxes

Hens prefer dark, enclosed nest boxes. A coop where the boxes are brightly lit invites prolonged time in the box and investigation. Hang a piece of curtain fabric over each opening, leaving only enough space for a hen to enter. The darker the interior, the less time hens spend investigating after laying.
Dealing with a committed egg eater

If one hen is clearly responsible (you catch her in the act, or find her beak stained with yolk), isolating her for a week while the other hens continue without broken eggs can break the flock's association. On return, ensure the root causes are addressed and rollaway boxes are fitted.

A hen with a fully established egg-eating habit that persists after all the above changes is generally culled. There is no reliably effective deterrent that works on a determined egg eater in a standard nest box.
Prevention from the start
Clean nest litter, adequate calcium year-round, and eggs collected twice daily prevent egg eating from starting in most flocks. The
weekly coop clean
keeps on top of the basic housekeeping.
